coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of photoluminescence carbon dots from red beetroot and utilizing its extract as blue-emitted fluorescence probes for spectrofluorimetric determination of tenoxicam in varied pharmaceutical samples
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Talal Al-Goul, Soha
Salem AlSalem, Huda
Saad Binkadem, Mona
El Hamd, Mohamed A.
Alsaggaf, Wejdan T.
Saleh, Safaa F.
Sallam, Sahar
Abdel-Lateef, Mohamed A.
coredata.dc:description: Tenoxicam (TNX) belongs to non-steroidal drugs that effectively treat many inflammatory disorders. In this study, luminescent carbon dots were synthesized from red beetroot as a green fluorescence probe. The synthesized carbon dots' morphology, size, and spectroscopic features were investigated and characterized by spectrophotometric, FTIR, SEM, spectrofluorimetric, and TEM techniques. The synthesized carbon dots possessed an emission at 430 nm and an excitation at 366.5 nm. Meanwhile, TNX is a yellow-colored compound with a spectrophotometric absorbance peak at 370 nm. Based on the inner filter effect mechanism, the emission fluorescence intensity of the synthesized carbon dots has been markedly quenched upon the addition of TNX. In addition, the quenching in the fluorescence intensity was quantitively related to the concentration of TNX at the linear range of 0.25 - 2.5 µg/mL (r2 = 0.9989) with a LOD of 0.08 µg/mL and LOQ of 0.24 µg/mL. The second side of this study is based on enhancing the fluorescence intensity of the acetonitrile extract of red beetroot upon the addition of TNX solution. This enhancement was recorded at a fluorescence emission of 357 nm with a fluorescence excitation of 305.5 nm. In addition, the enhancement in the fluorescence intensity was quantitively related to the concentration of TNX at the linear range of 0.5 - 8.0 µg/mL (r2 = 0.9995) with a LOD of 0.16 µg/mL and LOQ of 0.49 µg/mL. The analytical conditions were well-optimized, and the developed method was validated according to ICH guidelines. Finally, the proposed spectrofluorimetric approaches were applied to determine TNX in vials, tablets, and capsules with good recovery and accepted statistical results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Beta vulgaris
Tenoxicam
Carbon dots
Pharmaceutical analysis
Fluorescence assay

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Using the prepared carbon dots (CD method)
Using the extract of b. Vulgaris (EX-BV method)
Spectral and optical characterization of b. Vulgaris extract
Stability of B. Vulgaris extract and carbon dots
Fluorescence response of B. Vulgaris extract and carbon dots towards TNX
Mechanisms of sensing TNX by b. Vulgaris extract and carbon dots
Effect of pH on both sensing methods for determining TNX
Effect of diluting solvent on determining TNX by EX-BV method
Linear ranges and LOQ & LOD values
Determination of TNX in different pharmaceutical samples

The authors would like to thank the Deanship of Scientific Research at Shaqra University for supporting this work. Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University Researchers Supporting Project number (PNURSP2023R185), Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia
The authors would like to thank the Deanship of Scientific Research at Shaqra University for supporting this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Spectral and optical characterization of beta vulgaris acetonitrile extract; (A) for excitation and emission fluorescence spectra, (B) for spectrophotometric UV-absorbance spectrum, and (C) for optical images under daylight (I), and UV lamp (II).

Spectral and optical characterization of the prepared carbon dots; (A) for excitation and emission fluorescence spectra, (B) for spectrophotometric UV-absorbance spectrum, and (C) for optical images under daylight (I), and UV lamp (II).
Size, morphology, and surface functional groups characterization of the prepared carbon dots; (A) for TEM image, (B) for SEM image, and (C) for FTIR spectrum.
Spectra for determining TNX by the proposed methods; (A) for quenching the FI of carbon dots by TNX (2.0 µg/mL), and (B) for enhancing the FI of B. vulgaris by TNX (6.0 µg/mL).

(A): for absorbance spectrum of TNX in DMF/phosphoric acid solution, (B): for the effect of pH on fluorescence intensity (FIo) of carbon dots (blue line), on quenching the fluorescence intensity (FIo-FI) of carbon dots upon adding TNX (2.0 µg/mL, red line), and on enhancement in the fluorescence intensity (FIo) of B. vulgaris extract upon adding TNX (6.0 µg/mL, green line), and (C): effect of diluting solvent on the (FIo) fluorescence intensity of B. vulgaris extract upon adding TNX (6.0 µg/mL).
TNX molecular structures of three different forms: (A) for keto form, (B) for enol form, and (C) for zwitterionic.
Suggested mechanism for the reaction of TNX with betalain pigment via ion binary complex formation.
Analytical parameters for the suggested spectrofluorimetric methods.
Accuracy data for the suggested spectrofluorimetric methods.
Two levels of precision data for the suggested spectrofluorimetric methods.
Determination of TNX in different commercial samples by the suggested spectrofluorimetric methods.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *: Tabulated value at 95% confidence limit; F = 6.338 and t = 2.306.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of photoluminescence carbon dots from red beetroot and utilizing its extract as blue-emitted fluorescence probes for spectrofluorimetric determination of tenoxicam in varied pharmaceutical samples

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of photoluminescence carbon dots from red beetroot and utilizing its extract for spectrofluorimetric determination of tenoxicam.

Tenoxicam (TNX) belongs to non-steroidal drugs that effectively treat many inflammatory disorders. In this study, luminescent carbon dots were synthesized from red beetroot as a green fluorescence probe. The synthesized carbon dots' morphology, size, and spectroscopic features were investigated and characterized by spectrophotometric, FTIR, SEM, spectrofluorimetric, and TEM techniques. The synthesized carbon dots possessed an emission at 430 nm and an excitation at 366.5 nm. Meanwhile, TNX is a yellow-colored compound with a spectrophotometric absorbance peak at 370 nm. Based on the inner filter effect mechanism, the emission fluorescence intensity of the synthesized carbon dots has been markedly quenched upon the addition of TNX. In addition, the quenching in the fluorescence intensity was quantitively related to the concentration of TNX at the linear range of 0.25 - 2.5 µg/mL (r2 = 0.9989) with a LOD of 0.08 µg/mL and LOQ of 0.24 µg/mL. The second side of this study is based on enhancing the fluorescence intensity of the acetonitrile extract of red beetroot upon the addition of TNX solution. This enhancement was recorded at a fluorescence emission of 357 nm with a fluorescence excitation of 305.5 nm. In addition, the enhancement in the fluorescence intensity was quantitively related to the concentration of TNX at the linear range of 0.5 - 8.0 µg/mL (r2 = 0.9995) with a LOD of 0.16 µg/mL and LOQ of 0.49 µg/mL. The analytical conditions were well-optimized, and the developed method was validated according to ICH guidelines. Finally, the proposed spectrofluorimetric approaches were applied to determine TNX in vials, tablets, and capsules with good recovery and accepted statistical results.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Rheumatic diseases are a leading cause of disability and morbidity. Among rheumatic diseases is osteoarthritis, which is a painful disease and a precursor to disability. Drug regimens for managing the pain of osteoarthritis started with acetaminophen, followed by non-steroidal anti-inflammatory medications. Tenoxicam (TNX) is a long-acting non-steroidal anti-inflammatory medication that belongs to the oxicam family. TNX is defined by its powerful, antipyretic, anti-inflammatory, and analgesic effects [1]. Consequently, TNX effectively treats many inflammatory diseases involving musculoskeletal and joint diseases such as osteoarthritis, gout, rheumatoid arthritis, backache, ankylosing spondylitis, bursitis, tendonitis, etc. [2–4]. TNX is commercially formulated in several pharmaceutical dosage forms, such as capsules, tablets, and injectable vials.

In the literature, HPLC methods [5–10], TLC methods [11,12], flow injection spectrophotometric methods [13–15], and spectrophotometric [16,17] and electrochemical methods [18,19] were reported for determining TNX. Generally, the chromatographic methods require organic solvents, sophisticated procedures and equipment, and long sample preparation and analysis time, which makes their usage more inconvenient. Despite the user-friendly and simplicity of the spectrofluorimetric technique, only one spectrofluorimetric method has been reported for determining TNX and its potential impurity (2-aminopyridine) [20]. In addition to its sensitivity, accuracy, and selectivity over other spectroscopic techniques, the spectrofluorimetric technique is also characterized over the chromatographic methodologies by low-cost instrumentation; no pretreatment is needed, and less time and organic solvent in the analytical procedures [21–23].

In the last decade, carbon dots have attracted considerable attention for researchers in many fields, such as biomedicine, catalysis, anticounterfeiting, optoelectronic devices, and analytical chemistry due to their interesting and tunable photoluminescence, good hydrophilicity, good quantum yield, low toxicity, abundant & low-cost sources appreciable and biocompatibility [24]. Generally, carbon dots are defined as carbon-based nanomaterial with a nano-size below 20 nm, and luminescence is their inherent characteristic. Carbon dots have been widely utilized and applied as analytical fluorescent probes to detect various analytes such as dyes [25,26], metals[27], active pharmaceutical ingredients [28,29],…etc., due to their native fluorescent properties, quick response, high sensitivity, cost-effective, and simple preparation procedures. Furthermore, carbon dots considered as environmentally friendly than traditionally utilized fluorescent probes, such as synthetic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots[28].

Theoretically, the mechanisms for sensing analyte involve fluorescence resonance energy transfer, photoinduced electron transfer, inner filter effect mechanism (IFEM),..etc.[30]. The inner filter effect is a typical pattern in the spectrofluorimetric analysis, which only needs an adequate spectral overlapping between the absorbance band of the detected absorber (analyte) and the emission/ excitation band of the utilized fluorophore (reagent)[30]. Accordingly, the IFE-based strategy does not need the link of the analyte (absorber) with the reagent (fluorophore), which provides great simplicity and considerable flexibility[31]. Therefore, IFEM is considered an efficient and convenient strategy for designing novel fluorescence sensors[31,32].

Meanwhile, in the IFE-based strategy, the absorption signals have been converted into fluorescence signals, so enhancement in both sensitivity and selectivity can be successfully achieved [33,34]. Recently, the using inner filter effect strategy was reported for determining several kinds of active pharmaceutical ingredients such as methotrexate[35], vitamin B12 [36], carbamazepine[32],…etc.

Beetroot, or beta vulgaris, belongs to the family of Chenopodiaceae. Beetroot is a Middle Eastern plant, spread worldwide from Asia to Europe and the Americas, with numerous nutrition and health benefits [37]. It is a root vegetable with many constituents, such as sugars, flavonoids, nitrates, vitamins, and minerals, such as calcium, copper, potassium, iron, magnesium, phosphorous, sodium, manganese, and zinc [38]. In addition, beetroot consists of pigments (water-soluble) betalains [38]. Betalains pigment possesses a native fluorescence activity as an inherent characteristic[39]. Accordingly, we aim to try various solvents to extract beta vulgaris root, studying its fluorescence properties and using its extract as a natural fluorescence reagent for determining TNX in its various pharmaceutical samples based on IFEM. In addition, we aim to prepare fluorescent carbon dots from beta vulgaris root biomasses for the first time, studying their fluorescence properties and utilizing them as fluorescence reagents for determining TNX based on IFEM.

Beta vulgaris (B. vulgaris) roots were brought from a local market in Assiut City, Assiut, Egypt. Sodium hydroxide, acetone, ethanol, phosphoric acid, acetonitrile, citric acid, DMF, hydrochloric acid, methanol, and potassium hydroxide were manufactured by Elnaser Co. Cario, Egypt. Tenoxicam powder was kindly obtained from EIPICO Co., Egypt. Epicotil® 20 mg tablets (batch NO of 2201372) and Epicotil® 20 mg vials (batch NO of 2209184) were manufactured of EIPICO Co., Egypt. Soral® 20 mg capsules (batch NO of J23505/B) were manufactured by of Global Napi Pharmaceuticals Co., Egypt. All pharmaceutical samples were claimed and labeled to contain 20 mg of TNX per pharmaceutical dose (tablet, capsule, or vial). Furthermore, all samples were brought from the local Egyptian market. Distilled water type was used in this work.

Biomasses from B. vulgaris roots were carefully cleaned from clay, washed with water, and sliced into pieces. Pieces from B. vulgaris were softly blended in an electric blender with a small sufficient amount of absolute ethanol. The resulting minced biomasses were divided into equal portions, each portion equal to 50 mL, and separately transferred and added into 250-mL flasks. Each flask content was independently extracted with a particular solvent as 100 mL of absolute ethanol, 100 mL of methanol, 100 mL of acetone, 100 mL of DMF, and 100 mL of acetonitrile. The flask's content was constantly vortexed for 10 min several times and stored for 48 h in a dark place. The contents were separately filtered, and the filtrates were in the refrigerator until use.

Biomasses from B. vulgaris roots were carefully cleaned from clay, washed with water, and sliced into pieces. Pieces from B. vulgaris were softly blended in an electric blender with an adequate amount of water. 100 mL from the minced B. vulgaris roots was transferred into a 400 mL beaker, mixed with the proper amount from the concentrated orthophosphoric acid solution (about 10 mL), and heated at 200C for seven hours. The content should be diluted with a suitable amount of distilled water to remain hydrated as needed. The beaker content was allowed to cool, and the resulting content was diluted with distilled water to double volume, filtered through filter paper, and centrifuged. The supernatant was kept in the refrigerator until use.

FS2 spectrofluorometer (Scinco Co., Korea) was used to record all spectrofluorimetric measurements. JSM 5400 LV microscopical device (JEOL Co. Japan) was used for the SEM image, and JEM-100CX II microscopical device (JEOL Co, Japan) was used for the TEM image. A jenwey 350 pH meter, UV-1601 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Co., Japan), and Nicolet™ 6700 (Thermo Scientific Co., USA) FTIR spectroscope were also used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Using the prepared carbon dots (CD method)
ce:section.ce:para: In 10-mL volumetric flasks, different concentrations of TNX standard solution (dissolved in DMF) falling in the range of 2.5–25.0 µg/mL and were mixed with 2.0 mL of carbon dots solution. The content was well shaken and thoroughly completed to 10 mL with water. The blank was done, and the quenching in the fluorescence intensity was recorded for the final solution at a fluorescence emission of 430 nm after an excitation of 366.5 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Using the extract of b. Vulgaris (EX-BV method)
ce:section.ce:para: In 10-mL volumetric flasks, different concentrations of TNX standard solution (dissolved in DMF) falling in the range of 5.0–80.0 µg/mL were mixed with 1.0 mL of Teorell & Stenhagen buffer (pH = 3.5) and 0.25 mL of the acetonitrile extract of B. vulgaris. The content was well shaken and thoroughly completed to 10 mL with water. The blank was done, and the enhancement in the fluorescence intensity (FI) of the blank was recorded for the final solution at a fluorescence emission of 357 nm after an excitation of 305.5 nm.

The powder contents of three vials of Epicotil® 20 mg vials were weighed and mixed well. Similarly, the powder contents of ten capsules of Soral® 20 mg capsules were evacuated, weighed, and mixed well. Finally, ten tablets from Epicotil® 20 mg tablets were weighed and finely powdered. Particular amounts from each powdered sample equivalent to 50 mg of TNX were separately weighted, individually transferred, and added to a series of 100-mL volumetric flasks. Each powdered sample was sonicated with an appropriate amount of DMF solution. Then flasks were completed to the mark with DMF. The content of each flask was separately filtered and furtherly diluted to the required concentration of TNX with DMF solution. Finally, the general analytical procedures of the CD method and EX-BV method were applied to five determinations from each pharmaceutical sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectral and optical characterization of b. Vulgaris extract
ce:section.ce:para: B. vulgaris was extracted with varied common solvents, including acetone, methanol, ethanol, DMF, and acetonitrile. Practically, we found that the highest native fluorescence was achieved by using acetonitrile as an extraction solvent. In addition, the acetonitrile extract of B. vulgaris exhibits a fluorescence emission spectrum with a peak band of 357 nm and a fluorescence excitation spectrum with a peak band of 305.5 nm, Fig. 1 (A). Besides that, the acetonitrile extract of B. vulgaris was spectrophotometrically measured with the maximum absorbance bands at 270, 290, and 480 nm, Fig. 1 (B). Finally, the acetonitrile extract of B. vulgaris exhibits an orange-yellow color under daylight and bright fluorescent vision under a UV lamp, Fig. 1 (C).

The aqueous solution of the prepared carbon dots exhibited a native fluorescence activity has an emission spectrum with a peak band at 430 nm and an excitation spectrum with a peak band at 366.5 nm, Fig. 2 (A). The UV-spectrophotometric spectrum, daylight image, and the image under the UV lamp of the prepared carbon dots were presented in Fig. 2 (B) and Fig. 2 (C), respectively. Furthermore, the carbon dots have a tiny size diameter below 10 nm and a spherical shape, as provided in the TEM image and SEM image in Fig. 3 (A) and Fig. 3 (B), respectively. The functional groups on the surface of the prepared carbon dots were identified by the FTIR technique. From the FTIR spectrum offered in Fig. 3 (C), we can conclude that the appearance of a broad and intense peak at 3420.60 cm−1 refers to the presence of an O‑H stretched surface associated with the phenolic group [40]. In addition, the appearance of a peak at 2925.01 cm−1 is associated with the C‑H stretching of the aromatic skeleton [40]. In addition, the strong absorbance band at 1636.19 cm−1 refers to the existence of the carbonyl group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of B. Vulgaris extract and carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The stability study for either B. vulgaris acetonitrile extract or the prepared carbon dots was performed over a long period of time. It was found that after a period of 30 days, the prepared carbon dots did not appear to be subject to any form of aggregation, and the solution maintained its homogeneity without any noticeable alterations, which promotes the superabundant surface functionalization of the carbon dots. In addition, the FI of either B. vulgaris acetonitrile extract or the carbon dots remains constant without any change, which verifies and establishes using both of them as robust fluorescence reagents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of B. Vulgaris extract and carbon dots towards TNX
ce:section.ce:para: The defined and specified emission/excitation fluorescence spectra of the prepared carbon dots make them a very robust fluorescence analytical probe for planning to construct a functional assay system. It turned out that the FI of the carbon dots solution was sensitive to TNX as the FI of the emission spectra was notably quenched upon the addition of TNX solution, Fig. 4 (A). In addition, as the concentration of TNX increased, the FI of the prepared carbon dots at 430 nm gradually decreased. On the other side, we practically turned out that the FI of B. vulgaris acetonitrile extract was significantly augmented at both emission and excitation spectra upon the addition of TNX solution, Fig. 4 (B). In addition, as the concentration of TNX increased, the FI of B. vulgaris acetonitrile extract at 357 nm gradually increased. Consequently, B. vulgaris acetonitrile extract or the carbon dots have the potential validity for their utilization as robust fluorescent probes for determining NTX either turn-on or turn-off their fluorescence intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanisms of sensing TNX by b. Vulgaris extract and carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: In spectrofluorimetric analysis, the inner filter effect sensing mechanism needs an adequate spectral overlapping between the absorbance band of the detected absorber (analyte) and the emission or excitation band of the utilized fluorophore (reagent) to achieve this sensing strategy [30].

Here, the carbon dots have a fluorescence excitation spectrum at 366.5 nm, and TNX (yellow-colored compound) has an absorbance spectrum with a maximum peak at 370 nm Fig. 5 (A), which has successfully overlapped with the emission spectrum of the prepared carbon dots. Experimentally, it turned out that the FI of the prepared carbon dots was markedly quenched upon adding TNX solution. Furthermore, the efficiency of quenching the FI of the prepared carbon dots by TNX was evaluated by Stern-Volmer analysis as [41]:
K
SV
Q
+ 1 =
FIofcarbondotsonly
FIofcarbondotsinthepresenceofTNX

Where
Q
is refers to the molar concentration of TNX and
K
SV
is refers to (Stern–Volmer) quenching constant. Statistically, it turned out that the above-mentioned fluorescence intensity ratio is a good linear correlation with the concentration of TNX (r = 0.994) with the quenching constant value of
K
SV
= 96061
M
- 1
.

On the other side, B. vulgaris extract contains betalains as a common fluorescent pigment [38,39]. Betalain contains a positive nitrogen atom in its skeleton. In contrast, TNX is an enolic acid compound with pKa values of 5.3 and 1.1 [42]. In addition, the keto-enol tautomer chemical structures of TNX can result in a zwitterionic state through the intramolecular proton transfer [43,44], Scheme 1 . Therefore, the suggested mechanism for the marked enhancement in the FI of B. vulgaris extract upon the addition of TNX occurred through electrostatic interaction to form an ion pair or an association complex between the positively charged nitrogen atom in B. vulgaris pigment and the negative charge of the acidic center in TNX, Scheme 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on both sensing methods for determining TNX
ce:section.ce:para: The native FI of the prepared carbon dots is gradually decreased with the increase in the value of pH (by adding conc. KOH solution). In addition, the maximum FI as well as the great quenching in FI of the prepared carbon dots was observed in the strongly acidic medium, Fig. 5 (B). On the other side, the enhancement in the FI of B. vulgaris extract upon the addition of TNX was studied in a wide range of pH using Teorell & Stenhagen buffer. It turned out that the maximum enhancement in the FI was obtained at the pH value of 3.5 Fig. 5 (B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of diluting solvent on determining TNX by EX-BV method
ce:section.ce:para: Different solvents, including ethanol, acetone, DMF, methanol, acetonitrile, and water, were tested in addition to different surfactants, including cationic surfactant (HTAB), anionic surfactant (SDS) and nonionic surfactant (Tween 20) were also tested. It turned out that the maximum enhancement in the FI was obtained with water, and the lowest FI was obtained with acetone. Furthermore, there is no improvement in the FI of the aqueous solution upon adding any type of surfactant for the tested experiments, Fig. 5 (C).

The developed fluorometric methods were validated according to ICH guidelines [45] in respect to linear ranges of the TNX concentration, accuracy, two levels of precision, selectivity study, and LOQ and LOD values [45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Linear ranges and LOQ & LOD values
ce:section.ce:para: The calibration curves for determining NTX were constructed and assigned by plotting either FI values or (FI°‑FI)/FI° for the EX-BV method and CD method, respectively, against the related concentrations of NTX. The derived regression equations are FI = 278.83C + 107.35 and (FI°‑FI)/FI° = 0.1613C + 0.0138 for the EX-BV method and CD method, respectively (Figure S1). Where FI in the first regression equation refers to the enhancement in the fluorescence intensity of B. vulgaris extract upon the addition of TNX, while FI° and FI in the second regression equation refer to values of emission fluorescence intensity of carbon dots in the absence and presence of TNX, respectively, and C in both equations is refer to the related concentration of TNX by µg/mL. Statistically, it turned out that the proposed methods were linear over ranges of 0.5 - 8.0 µg/mL and 0.25 - 2.5 µg/mL with r2 values of 0.9995 and 0.9989 for the EX-BV method and CD method, respectively. The statistical data and the calculated analytical parameters for the proposed are displayed in Table 1 .

Moreover, LOD & LOQ values for determining TNX by both methods were calculated from equations
L O D =
3.3 × S D
I n t e r c e p t
S l o p e
a n d L O Q =
10 × S D
I n t e r c e p t
S l o p e

The estimated LOQ values of TNX were 0.49 µg/mL and 0.24 µg/mL for the EX-BV method and CD method, respectively. In addition, the estimated LOD values of TNX were 0.16 µg/mL and 0.08 µg/mL for the EX-BV method and CD method, respectively.

At different concentration levels from standard TNX solution spread along the linear ranges of the EX-BV method and CD method, accuracy, inter-day and intra-day precision for both methods were investigated and assigned. The used TNX concentrations for the assessment of accuracy were 1.0, 3.0, and 7.0 µg/mL for the EX-BV method and 0.5, 1.0, and 2.5 µg/mL for the CD method, and results were interpreted as recovery ± SD. Meanwhile, the used TNX concentrations for the assessment of both inter-day and intra-day precision were 2. 0, 4.0, and 6.0 µg/mL for the EX-BV method and 1.0, 1.5, and 2.0 µg/mL for the CD method, and results were interpreted as recovery ± RSD. Table 2 and Table 3 exhibits the good accuracy and precision of the proposed methods for determining TNX as the obtained recovery ± SD and recovery ± RSD values are within the acceptable values, which proven accuracy and precision at two levels (inter-day & intra-day) for EX-BV method and CD method.

In the Egyptian market, TNX has been pharmaceutically formulated alone (not combined with other drugs). Therefore, the selectivity study was checked against common pharmaceutical excipients such as carboxymethylcellulose, starch, lactose, sucrose, sorbitol, dextrose, etc. All of these substances were spiked at the concentration level of 5 µg/mL. It turned out that neither of these substances can affect the determination of TNX by the EX-BV method or by the CD method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of TNX in different pharmaceutical samples
ce:section.ce:para: The EX-BV method and CD method were applied to analyze TNX content in various commercial types of pharmaceutical samples, including; Epicotil® tablets, Epicotil® vials, and Soral® 20 mg capsules. The calculated results for five determinations of TNX in each sample were interpreted as recovery ± SD. The obtained results for determining TNX were statistically compared with those of the reported spectrofluorimetric method [20]. It turned out that all values are within the accepted levels, Table 4 , which has proven the capability and reliability of the EX-BV method and CD method for determining TNX in different pharmaceutical samples.

For the first time, fluorescence carbon dots with specified excitation/emission spectra were prepared from red beetroot biomasses. The carbon dots possessed a fluorescence emission peak at 430 nm and an excitation at 366.5 nm. In addition, the red beetroot biomasses were extracted with different solvents, and acetonitrile extract exhibited a specified excitation/emission at 305.5/357 nm. Both the carbon dots and the extract of red beetroot successfully utilized fluorescence probes for developing two spectrofluorometric methods for determining tenoxicam. The first determination mechanism is based on the inner filter effect by switching off the fluorescence intensity of the prepared carbon dots by TNX. While the second determination mechanism is based on the enhancement of the fluorescence intensity of the red beetroot, extract through the formation of ion pair between TNX and the dye of red beetroot extract. Both analytical methods were optimized and validated according to ICH guidelines. In addition, both methods exhibited good capability and reliability for determining TNX in different pharmaceutical samples, including tablets, vials, and capsules, with acceptable results.

Soha Talal Al-Goul: Methodology, Investigation, Data Curation, Formal analysis, Writing – Original Draft. Huda Salem AlSalem: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – Review & Editing, Formal analysis, Validation, Funding acquisition. Mona Saad Binkadem: Writing – Review & Editing, Methodology, Investigation, Resources, Formal analysis, Validation. Mohamed A. El Hamd: Validation, Data Curation, Formal analysis, Software. Wejdan T. Alsaggaf: Writing – Review & Editing, Formal analysis. Safaa F. Saleh: Writing – Review & Editing, Software. Sahar Sallam: Data Curation, Writing – Review & Editing. Mohamed A. Abdel-Lateef: Methodology, Conceptualization, Investigation, Resources, Writing – Original Draft, Visualization, Data Curation, Validation, Supervision, Writing – Review & Editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University Researchers Supporting Project number (PNURSP2023R185), Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.
The authors would like to thank the Deanship of Scientific Research at Shaqra University for supporting this work.

ce:section.ce:para: Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University Researchers Supporting Project number (PNURSP2023R185), Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2023.115028.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
